explanation: I was tracking my sleep and its impact on athletic performance. There are three main categories im tracking for athletic performance. Mile time, 40 yard dash, And vertical jump. I want to see will more sleep lead to better or consistent performance. There are other factors to consider like diet but the main thing being measured is sleep and performance. I learned that i have no consistent night time routine, each sleep goal i've set i rarely meet. My method of collection has been my apple watch and my stop watch. Barriers- diet has been affected due to fasting i don't know if its affecting my performance.

I also learned my sleep schedule is also not consistent on reaching 7 hours per day.

Reflection: Since my last reflection i have noticed what's been contributing to my lack of sleep. It's been my screen time and the hours i am eating food. I will start decreasing my screen time and eat fuller meals to prevent late night snacking. That way i can make progress on a consistent schedule. I've started to set new guidelines Set consistent bedtime/wake-up Implement pre-sleep routine maintain sleep log; monitor diet before workouts compare performance trends to sleep patterns Adjust bedtime, meals, or training times if needed

Since last week, I continued tracking my sleep duration, energy levels, and bench press performance to see if any patterns changed . I noticed that on days when I slept around 7–9 hours, my energy levels were generally higher and my bench press performance improved. This additional week of data helped show the trend that getting more sleep may positively impact both energy and gym performance.

Sleep Trends: My sleep ranged from 4–9 hours, with the first two weeks showing lower consistency. Week 3 and 4 showed more stable sleep habits, with most nights averaging 6–8 hours. Performance Trends:Mile times fluctuated early on but became more consistent as sleep stabilized. Vertical jump height showed slight improvement in weeks with better sleep, though occasional outliers occurred. 40-yard dash times showed modest improvement, particularly when I maintained a regular sleep routine. Sleep vs. Performance: While not a perfect correlation, patterns indicate that nights with 7–8 hours of sleep generally corresponded to faster mile times and slightly higher vertical jumps. Scatter plots of sleep vs. mile times and vertical jump reveal this relationship clearly. Experimentation & Lessons Learned: I implemented structured routines (9 PM wind-down, last meal by 8:30 PM, and limiting screen time) which improved sleep consistency. Adjusting diet and timing of meals also helped reduce late-night disruptions. Early in the project, inconsistent data collection and distractions were barriers; regular logging with Apple Watch and stopwatch helped overcome them.
